text: This video will demonstrate a right flank incisional hernia repair using the ETEP reef stopa with right tar technique. The incisional hernia was eight centimeters wide and six centimeters long. The strategy was to place three ports to the right of the right linea alba, dissect to the linea semiliners, perform a tar to access the retroperitoneal space.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position with an axillary roll. An ultrasound is used to identify the right linea alba and the right linea semilunaris, guiding the optical entry into the epigastrium to the right of the linea alba.

The surgeon uses an applied medical key files trocar to enter the right retro rectus space, sweeping fibril fatty areolar tissue away from the posterior sheath. Two robotic ports are placed under direct visualization, and the initial five-millimeter port is upsized to an eight-millimeter robotic port. The epigastric eight-millimeter robotic port is later upsized to twelve millimeters to accommodate the placement of a heavyweight mesh.

The surgeon dissects towards the linea semilunaris, revealing a large neurovascular bundle in the retrorectus space. The dissection progresses from the right lower quadrant to the right upper quadrant. The surgeon begins the transversus abdominis release (TAR) by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and dividing a medial neurovascular bundle. The transverse abdominus muscle is then divided to access the pre-transversalis fascia plane.

The surgeon navigates between the transversus abdominis muscle and the transverse salus fascia, attempting to separate these layers. A small perforation in the peritoneum is visible, causing CO₂ pressure equalization. Despite this, the retrorectus space remains adequately developed. The surgeon continues the TAR caudally, where the muscle transitions to a more aponeurotic form, facilitating separation using the ETAP technique.

The surgeon continues the dissection for a complete transverse abdominus release, moving towards the hernia sac. The dissection progresses under the diaphragm and extends below the hernia sac into the right groin area.

The surgeon divides the aponeurotic portion of the posterior sheath medial to the linea semilunaris, near the hernia sac. Fat tissue is visible below the level of the hernia sac as the dissection progresses into the right lower quadrant.

The assistant applies pressure on the hernia as the surgeon flips the scope to work around the hernia neck. The surgeon carefully maneuvers to stay outside the hernia sac, attempting to reduce the sac and its contents without entering the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon is at the neck of the hernia sac, attempting to find a plane between the hernia sac and surrounding structures to reduce it. The hernia sac is minimal, with a thin piece of peritoneum visible. Muscle is being carefully dissected off the hernia sac, eventually leading to entry into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attempts to peel the extremely thin hernia sac away from the hernia walls. Upon opening the sac, pericolonic fat is visible. The right colon, previously seen within the hernia on a CT scan, is reduced as the surgeon works to free it from the sac, noting that the adhesions are not extensive.

The surgeon uses a wristed robotic instrument to maneuver around the corners of the hernia site, snipping adhesive bands tethering the hernia. Bare muscle striations are visible in the background. The hernia sac is minimal, with only one adhesion present. The surgeon proceeds to divide the neck of the hernia and continues lateral dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral portion of the hernia sac, staying close to the peritoneum. The dissection aims to mobilize the neck of the hernia sac while avoiding lateral movement into fatty tissue near the nerves from the right psoas muscle. Two distinct tissue layers are visible as the surgeon finds a suitable plane for dissection.

The surgeon uses their left hand to retract the posterior perirenal fascia towards themselves, revealing the lateral conal fascia. The fascia appears intact without a discrete defect, and the surgeon aims to achieve extensive overlap with the mesh, particularly in the posterior lateral area.

The surgeon navigates through scar tissue, attempting to maintain the established tissue plane between the posterior perirenal fascia and the lateral conal fascia. Careful manipulation of the tissue is performed to avoid damaging the kidney or ureter, with visible remnants of the tissue plane aiding in orientation.

The surgeon connects the dissection to the previously developed plane in the upper portion of the transversus abdominis release (TAR). The patient is positioned in straight lateral decubitus, allowing for medial rotation of the peritoneum and colon, facilitating exposure to the medial edge of the psoas muscle.

The surgeon enters a pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen or upper flank, approaching the area where the kidney is located. The dissection reveals distinct areolar tissue that guides the surgical path.

The psoas muscle becomes visible as the surgeon carefully dissects anterior to it, preserving the psoas fascia to avoid injuring nearby nerves such as the genitofemoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, iliohypogastric, and ilioinguinal nerves. The area shows significant scarring from a previous partial nephrectomy, with the kidney being retracted medially by the surgeon's left hand. A Weck clip from the nephrectomy is encountered during the dissection.

The surgeon uses a wrist sit instrument to navigate around the kidney, carefully working through dense scar tissue. The firm structure within the scarred fatty tissue is visible, though its specific identity is uncertain.

The surgeon identifies a white Weck clip while positioned very lateral to the incisional hernia. Despite being at a point where dissection could stop, the surgeon continues to ensure good overlap with a heavyweight mesh, considering the patient's high BMI.

The surgeon observes a small peritoneal hole near the lateral portion of the liver and leaves it in place. A groove extends from the right pelvis to the subdiaphragmatic space, prepared for mesh placement. The main defect measures six centimeters cranially and eight centimeters laterally, with visible lateral weakness but no true hernia.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal hole, identified as the neck of the hernia sac, using a 3-0 suture. The main defect is visible, lacking a significant hernia sac. The surgeon uses an OV lock suture technique, laying out the sutures to manage slack and progressively tightening them to close the defect.

The surgeon decreases the pneumoperitoneum to approximately eight millimeters mercury pressure to prevent muscle tears. Two muscle layers are visible, likely the transverse abdominis and internal oblique, showing a defect. The surgeon uses their left hand to brace the tissue while pulling the suture with the right hand to avoid cutting through the tissue.

The surgeon identifies a large hole beneath the external oblique muscle and places a 5mm port under direct vision into the space. A 19 French JP drain is threaded through the port into the cavity to prevent seroma formation, given the large cavity size. The closure involves muscle-to-muscle approximation without significant fascia.

The surgeon places heavy weight mesh with good overlap over the suture line, noting minimal tension medially and laterally but some tension centrally. A second row of sutures is run to offload tension from the initial row. The lateral area appears weak but is covered with mesh, ensuring no discrete defect is visible.

The surgeon examines the space for mesh placement, identifying two spaces: one between the peritoneum and the lateral conal fascia, and another between the transverse salus and the transverse abdominus. The anterior superior iliac spine and iliac crest are located, with the mesh planned to be positioned slightly below the iliac crest. The space measures approximately 25 centimeters long by 26 centimeters wide. A heavyweight barred mesh is prepared for placement through a 12 millimeter port.

The surgeon measures the heavyweight mesh to fit into the groove anterior to the psoas muscle, ensuring good caudal overlap. The posterior sheath's cut edge is visible during further dissection. The optical entry port is upsized to a twelve millimeter for mesh placement. The surgeon attempts to retract the kidney, noting its unexpected heaviness compared to the effortless lateral dissection performed by the robot.

The surgeon tucks the posterior lateral edge of the heavyweight mesh into the groove adjacent to the psoas muscle. The mesh overlaps laterally by approximately ten centimeters. A suture technique is employed to secure the long piece of mesh in place.
This patient stayed in the hospital overnight and was discharged on post-op day one. Thank you for watching.
